coredata.dc:title: Glycopeptide-mimetic carbon dots/graphene oxide mediated fluorescent-recovery sensor for detecting Salmonella typhimurium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xuan
Lin, Zhenhao
Cai, Jihai
Wang, Xiaoying
coredata.dc:description: Food safety issues associated with Salmonella present serious health risks, making detection crucial for public health protection. This study develops an efficient strategy for the capture of Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) based on the affinity between glycopeptide-mimetic carbon dots (g-CDs) and the type I fimbriae of S. typhimurium. The g-CDs were obtained by carbonizing mannose and histidine, which can be stored without light protection for over 90 days. Specially, the incorporation of histidine reduced the dissociation constant of g-CDs with lectin ConA from 135 μM to 25.4 μM. Further, g-CDs were anchored to phenylboronic acid-functionalized graphene oxide (GO-PBA) via dynamic borate bonds to create a fluorescent turn-on probe (CDs/GO-PBA). This probe exhibited a liner range of 104–107 CFU mL−1, achieving 2370 CFU mL−1 detection limit with a recovery rate ranging from 93 % to 103 % in chicken. The fluorescent sensor provides a promising strategy for the detection of S. typhimurium in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glycopeptide-mimetic CDs
Graphene oxide
Fluorescent detection
Salmonella typhimurium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimating affinity of g-CDs with S. typhimurium
Characterization of g-CDs, GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA
Detection of S. typhimurium by CDs/GO-PBA
Detection of S. typhimurium in chicken
Modulation the affinity of g-CDs with S. typhimurium
Sensing of S. typhimurium by CDs/GO-PBA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of CDs/GO-PBA and its application in S. typhimurium detection.

(A) Mechanism of g-CDs formation; (B) TEM image of g-CDs binding to S. typhimurium; (C) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of CDs-11 incubated with S. typhimurium (104–108 CFU mL−1) in saline (pH 7.2); (D) FL emission spectra of CDs-10 incubated with S. typhimurium (104–108 CFU mL−1) in saline (pH 7.2); (E) The linear fitting curve of FL intensity of CDs (CDs-11 in blue and CDs-10 in green) with centrifugation of S. typhimurium; (F) Selectivity of CDs-11 responding to different bacteria at 108 CFU mL−1; (G) Zeta potential of CDs, S. typhimurium, and ConA.

ITC graphs of (A) CDs-11 and (B) CDs-10 titrations with Con A. The experimental data (solid squares) were fit to theoretical titrationcurves (solid lines) using the software supplied by the ITC manufacturer.

Characterizations of CDs-11: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra, FL excitation and emission spectra, the insets are photographs under visible light (left) and UV beam of 365 nm (right); (B) 3D FL spectra; (C) TEM image; (D) Particle size distribution; (E) FL decay spectrum; (F) XPS full scan spectrum; (G) C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum; (H) O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum; (I) N1s high-resolution XPS spectrum.

(A) TEM image of GO-PBA; (B) C-, O- and B- element mapping images of GO-PBA; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of GO and GO-PBA, the insets are physical images of GO (left) and GO-PBA (right); (D) FT-IR spectra of GO and GO-PBA.

(A) TEM image of CDs/GO-PBA; (B) EDS mapping images of CDs/GO-PBA; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra and (D) FT-IR spectra of CDs-11, GO-PBA, and CDs/GO-PBA; (E) XPS spectra of GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA; (F) C1s high-resolution XPS spectra; (G) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra; (H) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra; (I) B1s high-resolution XPS spectra.

(A) Sensing of S. typhimurium by CDs/GO-PBA; (B) FL spectra of CDs/GO-PBA before and after 1h of incubation with S. typhimurium, the inset is pictures of CDs/GO-PBA under 365 nm UV irradiation; (C) FL intensities of CDs/GO-PBA after different times of incubation; (D) FL spectra of CDs/GO-PBA in saline (pH 7.2) response to S. typhimurium (104–108 CFU mL−1); (E) FL response of CDs/GO-PBA to S. typhimurium (104–108 CFU mL−1); (F) SEM image of S. typhimurium bound to CDs/GO-PBA; (G) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of S. typhimurium incubated with CDs/GO-PBA: bright field image (left), FL image (mid), and merged image (right).
Binding performance of g-CDs with S. typhimurium prepared under different conditions.
Results of S. typhimurium detection in 10-fold-diluted chicken samples (n = 3).
Comparisons of the proposed sensor with recently reported methods for detection of S. typhimurium.

head.ce:title: Glycopeptide-mimetic carbon dots/graphene oxide mediated fluorescent-recovery sensor for detecting Salmonella typhimurium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food safety issues associated with Salmonella present serious health risks, making detection crucial for public health protection. This study develops an efficient strategy for the capture of Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) based on the affinity between glycopeptide-mimetic carbon dots (g-CDs) and the type I fimbriae of S. typhimurium. The g-CDs were obtained by carbonizing mannose and histidine, which can be stored without light protection for over 90 days. Specially, the incorporation of histidine reduced the dissociation constant of g-CDs with lectin ConA from 135 μM to 25.4 μM. Further, g-CDs were anchored to phenylboronic acid-functionalized graphene oxide (GO-PBA) via dynamic borate bonds to create a fluorescent turn-on probe (CDs/GO-PBA). This probe exhibited a liner range of 104–107 CFU mL−1, achieving 2370 CFU mL−1 detection limit with a recovery rate ranging from 93 % to 103 % in chicken. The fluorescent sensor provides a promising strategy for the detection of S. typhimurium in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Salmonella is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium classified within the Enterobacteriaceae family. Among 2500 different serotypes of this family, Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) is the most prevalent pathogen found in humans. Ingesting these bacteria can cause salmonellosis, with symptoms including fever, abdominal pain, nausea and diarrhea [1]. Therefore, the association of S. typhimurium with outbreaks of diseases linked to contaminated chicken, pork, milk, eggs and various other foods underscores the critical need for early and rapid detection of this pathogen to ensure food safety and quality.

Currently, bacterial sensing predominantly employs biological recognizers, such as antibodies and aptamers, etc., in conjunction with fluorescent dyes. However, the application of these methods is still limited by certain drawbacks. Fluorescent dyes exhibit high background noise and are susceptible to photobleaching [2]. Antibodies, being proteins, may exhibit poor stability and high price. Additionally, while DNA-based aptamers offer better stability than antibodies, they still need to be stored in refrigerator at −20oC, unlike carbon dots, which do not require such conditions. This characteristic of carbon dots brings great convenience to the detection of pathogenic bacteria when they are applied as probe. It is noted that carbohydrates exhibit special binding affinity with bacteria, characterized by their non-immunogenic and thermally stable properties. For instance, mannose specifically targets the FimH lectin on type I fimbriae of S. typhimurium through multiple non-covalent interactions [3–5]. This performance positions mannose as a promising alternative to aptamers and antibodies for bacterial recognition. Nevertheless, the carbohydrate-lectin interactions are weak and characterized by high dissociation constants (470 μM), requiring enhancement to improve sensitivity [6]. Moreover, mannose lacks inherent signaling capabilities, requiring modification with signaling molecules for effective sensing.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a carbon core and functional groups on surface which exhibit excellent luminescence performance with good photostability. These characteristics effectively mitigate the limitations associated with the poor photostability of traditional dyes, leading to increased interest in their application for pathogen detection [7–9]. Moreover, the ability to easily modify surfaces and retain functional groups from precursor materials allows the CDs to be engineered with specific ligands for enhanced sensing capabilities [10]. In this way, it will be very interesting that mannose is carbonized to obtain CDs, which can serve as a versatile platform for clustered presentation, enhancing binding with FimH through multivalent interactions. Additionally, the fluorescence properties of CDs offer a reliable signal output, effectively resolving the issue that mannose lacks detectable physical signal for rapid sensing. Consequently, CDs are anticipated to replace conventional fluorescent dyes in bacterial detection methods. Furthermore, saccharides and peptides are important molecular partners in many biological systems, often binding covalently with each other to form glycopeptides with functional biological activity [6]. It is reported that glycopeptide-mimics show enhanced interaction with bacterial and viral surfaces and have potential applications in pathogen diagnosis and drug development [11,12]. Therefore, it is fascinating to construct a glycopeptide-mimetic carbon dots (g-CDs) to improve affinity for S. typhimurium. The incorporation of amino acid residues on g-CDs may modulates binding affinity by their nature of hydrophilicity-hydrophobicity and acid-base, resulting in providing additional interactions to further enhance binding. Additionally, the introduction of nitrogen increased the quantum yield of g-CDs and their fluorescence response to S. typhimurium.

Nevertheless, the turn-off sensing through removing g-CDs bound to Salmonella is disturbed by high intensity of initial fluorescence, and sensitivity can be enhanced by altering the fluorescence response mode [13]. Graphene oxide (GO) has been reported as an efficient quencher for a variety of fluorescent nanomaterials. The stacking of g-CDs onto surface of GO can result in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from g-CDs to GO and fluorescence quenching [14]. Furthermore, the binding of g-CDs to GO can be facilitated by phenylboronic acid (PBA), as the diol bond of PBA forms a dynamic covalent bond with the diol groups present in sugars such as glucose, mannose, and galactose. Previous studies have demonstrated the effective detection of glucose using GO-PBA [15–17]. Therefore, if the diol bonds on the mannose residues of g-CDs react with PBA to form a dynamic borate bond, a fluorescence-recovering probe will be constructed, in which g-CDs detach from the GO surface in the presence of S. typhimurium, leading to the recovery of fluorescent signals.

Herein, as shown in Fig. 1 , this study presents a one-step, cost-effective fluorescence-recovery probe for the detection of foodborne S. typhimurium by employing GO-PBA as the quenching agent and g-CDs specifically designed for S. typhimurium as the signal source. The g-CDs, synthesized by mannose and histidine, possess a glycopeptide-mimetic structure that enables selective binding with S. typhimurium. Furthermore, GO and 3-APBA were reacted via amidation to introduce boric acid group which was capable of dynamic binding to the diol moieties of g-CDs. This interaction resulted in fluorescence quenching of g-CDs. In the presence of S. typhimurium, the borate bonds between g-CDs and GO-PBA were disrupted due to the interaction of g-CDs with lectins of S. typhimurium, resulting in fluorescence recovery of g-CDs. The performance of CDs/GO-PBA biosensor was evaluated by testing bacterial cultures and chicken samples, demonstrating its potential as a tool for detecting foodborne bacteria.

Mannose, histidine, and 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-APBA) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Scalar graphite (325 mesh) was obtained from Nanjing Xianfeng Nanomaterials Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth medium was bought from Guangzhou Huankai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 14028) was supplied by Guangdong Microbial Culture Collection Center (Guangzhou, China). All other chemicals were analytical reagent grade and were used as received.

The g-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.4 g of mannose and amino acids in different ratios were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water., The reaction temperature was raised to a predetermined value and followed by a hydrothermal reaction for a specified duration. The g-CDs were purified through dialysis with deionized water. The final solution was freeze-dried to obtain g-CDs powder. A series of g-CDs were obtained by varying the hydrothermal temperature, reaction time, the feeding ratio of mannose to amino acids, and the types of amino acids (histidine, serine, aspartic acid, phenylalanine).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimating affinity of g-CDs with S. typhimurium
ce:section.ce:para: S. typhimurium was cultured in LB broth at 37 °C for 20 h, it was then collected by centrifugation and resuspended in sterilized saline (0.9 % NaCl, pH 7.2). The population size was determined by measuring absorbance at 600 nm (OD600). The number of colony forming units per milliliter (CFU·mL−1) was calculated by counting colonies on agar plate. A standard curve of OD600 versus bacterial counts was established (concentration (CFU·mL−1) = 2.79 × 109 × OD600 - 1.20 × 108 (R2 = 0.999), Fig. S1) for subsequent S. typhimurium concentration calibration [18]. The affinity of g-CDs was estimated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant after incubating with various concentrations of S. typhimurium for 1 h.

The preparation and modification of GO were based on previous studies [16,19]. In this process, 25 mg of 3-APBA was added to 8 mL of 0.5 mg mL−1 GO solution and stirred vigorously at 70 °C for 3 h. Unreacted 3-APBA was removed by centrifugation and washed to obtain GO-PBA. After adjusting the pH of GO-PBA solution to 8.0, g-CDs were added and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was centrifuged to remove unreacted g-CDs, yielding the CDs/GO-PBA nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of g-CDs, GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100F) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, Germany) were used to observe the morphology of g-CDs, GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA. The UV–Vis spectra were obtained on a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were measured by a Tensor 27 IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity were determined through an F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The chemical composition of materials was investigated by energy dispersive X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a ESCALAB 250 instrument (Thermo-VG Scientific, America). The Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC) of g-CDs were carried out on Agilent Technologies 1260 Infinity (Agilent, America) by elution with PBS (pH 7.4), calibrated by external standard species (dextran, DXT) for molecular weight (Mw) determination in the 100–5250 range. Quantum yields (QY) of g-CDs were evaluated by measuring the ratio between fluorescence and absorbance at a fixed wavelength by comparison with quinine sulfate [20]. The zeta potentials of g-CDs, S. typhimurium, and Concanavalin A (ConA) were determined by Malvern zeta potential meter, and the binding constants of g-CDs with ConA were investigated by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). Content of mannose in g-CDs was measured by phenol sulfate method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. typhimurium by CDs/GO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of CDs/GO-PBA, suspensions of S. typhimurium were prepared at concentrations ranging from 1 × 104 to 1 × 107 CFU mL−1. For testing, 500 μL of a 0.3 mg mL−1 CDs/GO-PBA solution was mixed with 1 mL of bacterial suspension (0.9 % NaCl solution containing 1 mM Ca2+ and Mn2+). After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h with slight shaking, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was measured at 430 nm under excitation at 360 nm. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by dividing three times the standard deviation (SD) by the slope of linear equation.

Additionally, confocal fluorescence microscopy (LSM 880, Zeiss, Germany) was used to observe the binding of g-CDs to S. typhimurium. The mixed incubation solution of CDs/GO-PBA with S. typhimurium was centrifuged, and the resulting precipitate was fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution at 37 °C for 2 h. After fixation, it was washed successively with 50 %, 60 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % ethanol. Finally, 20 μL of the dispersion was dropped on a silico wafer and dried for SEM characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S. typhimurium in chicken
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the method for detecting S. typhimurium in real samples, chicken samples were purchased from a local supermarket and pretreated according to Chinese National Food Safety Standards (GB 4789.2–2022). The samples were subsequently contaminated with S. typhimurium at concentrations ranging from 104 to 107 CFU mL−1. Then, these chicken samples were tested by the above method, with standard samples serving as control. Each sample was tested in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modulation the affinity of g-CDs with S. typhimurium
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare g-CDs with enhanced affinity with S. typhimurium, different conditions including temperature, time, ratio and species of amino acids were systematically evaluated. The mannose content, molecular weight and quantum yield of g-CDs were measured under these varying conditions. The mechanism of g-CDs formation is illustrated in Fig. 2 A. Hydrothermal conditions facilitate the cross-linking and polymerization of groups between mannose and amino acids, leading to the initial formation of a monolithic unit. As the reaction progresses, carbonization occurs after the completion of polymerization. Carbonization results in the formation of carbon nuclei within the polymerized structure and a gradual loss of external groups. Therefore, g-CDs rich in surface mannose residues are essential for bacterial detection.

Mannose residues on the surface of the g-CDs specifically bind to FimH at the tips of Salmonella surface fimbriae [21]. In Fig. 2B, a significant attachment of g-CDs to the surface of S. typhimurium is evident. When separated by centrifugation, a decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed due to the decrease of g-CDs in the solution (Fig. 2C). The extent of fluorescence decrease was directly correlated with the binding affinity between g-CDs and S. typhimurium. Therefore, the detection performance of the g-CDs under different conditions was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant after incubation with S. typhimurium. The linear range of the assay, the fitted standard deviation (R2), and the slopes of the fitted curves are shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, temperature significantly influenced the binding ability of g-CDs to S. typhimurium, with optimal performance occurred at 190 °C. Amazingly, CDs-3 exhibited the capability to indicate varying concentrations of S. typhimurium, while other temperatures-regulated g-CDs (CDs-1, 2, 4, 5) responded to very high concentrations (107–108 CFU mL−1). The molecular weight of g-CDs initially increased with rising temperature and then decreased. This behavior was attributed to the processes of polymerization followed by carbonization of mannose and histidine constituents (Fig. S3A). Notably, the quantum yield of g-CDs gradually increased (from 3.78 % to 7.63 %) due to the escalation of carbonization (Fig. S3A). Regarding hydrothermal reaction time, CDs-8 (9 h hydrothermal time) exhibited the highest binding affinity for S. typhimurium, attaining a maximum standard deviation of 0.915 for the fitting curve. From the FT-IR spectra, it was observed that the structure of carbon dots (CDs-1 to 9) did not change significantly at different hydrothermal temperatures and durations for the same mannose and histidine ratios (Figs. S4A and B). As shown in Fig. S3B, CDs-8 was the lowest molecular weight of 1006 and the quantum yield increased to 8.76 %. Additionally, the effect of the ratio of mannose to amino acids on binding affinity was evaluated. As the proportion of mannose increased, the affinity of g-CDs to S. typhimurium also rose, reaching a peak at a ratio of 7:1 (Table 1). The observed differences in the binding affinity of g-CDs with S. typhimurium may be attributed to the mannose content present on the surface of g-CDs. The mannose content of these g-CDs was quantified by the phenol-sulfuric acid method. Results indicated that the sugar content of g-CDs increased with rising hydrothermal temperatures within the range of 150–190 °C, peaking at 190 °C with a value of 46.06 ± 1.17 μg mg−1. However, exposure to higher temperatures (above 190 °C) resulted in carbonization of mannose on the surface of g-CDs, leading to a reduction in mannose content (CDs-4 recorded 25.16 ± 1.53 μg mg−1; CDs-5 recorded 24.17 ± 0.71 μg mg−1, as shown in Fig. S2A). The hydrothermal time had no obvious effect on the mannose content of g-CDs. The mannose content of g-CDs reached the maximum at 9 h, which was 51.84 ± 0.79 μg mg−1 (Fig. S2B). Furthermore, it was determined that proportion of mannose to histidine had a significant impact on sugar content levels. As shown in Fig. S2C, there was a decrease in mannose content corresponding with reductions in mannose proportion. The highest level of mannose (72.96 ± 0.79 μg mg−1) was achieved when employing a ratio of mannose to histidine set at 7:1, which also exhibited optimal binding efficacy towards S. typhimurium (Table 1). Specifically, the performance of g-CDs synthesized solely from mannose is inferior to that of g-CDs containing amino acids. Fig. 2C illustrates the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-11 upon binding to S. typhimurium at different concentrations. Compared to CDs-10 (Fig. 2D), although the performance of both is comparable within the detection range, there is a significant difference in the fluorescence signal changes. As shown in Fig. 2E, the absolute slope value of CDs-10 is only 27.4 CPS·CFU−1 mL, whereas the slope value of CDs-11 is 95.0 CPS·CFU−1 mL, indicating that CDs-11 exhibits a superior fluorescence response performance. This is due to the formation of glycopeptide-like structures between mannose and amino acids, with the nitrogen component of the amino acid enhancing the quantum yield of g-CDs (from 1.25 % to 7.02 %, Fig. S3C), thus improving fluorescence intensity sensitivity. When the ratio was varied, a peak at 1760 cm−1 attributed to the hexacyclic ketone CO stretching disappeared when the mannose content fell below 25 %. Additionally, with an increase of histidine content exceeding 75 %, new peaks appeared at 1340 cm−1 and 650 cm−1, attributed to C–N and N–H stretching, respectively. A peak at 940 cm−1 corresponded to O–H of the carboxylic acid was also observed, likely due to histidine residues (Fig. S4C). Furthermore, g-CDs with histidine showed the highest affinity compared to other amino acids, including serine, aspartic acid and phenylalanine (Table 1), which indicated an advantage of alkaline amino acid over acidic, neutral and aromatic counterparts. The varying contents of mannose in g-CDs synthesized by different amino acids likely arose from differences in their solubility and glycopeptide reactivity, which ultimately affected bacterial affinity (Fig. S2D). For instance, aspartic acid exhibited lower solubility compared to histidine, making it more challenging to interact with mannose. Consequently, it was difficult for aspartic acid to react directly with mannose, resulting in a higher mannose content. However, this process did not form an effective glycopeptide structure to enhance their binding affinity with S. typhimurium. Additionally, the reduced nitrogen doping resulted in a lower quantum yield and a diminished fluorescence signal response. In the FI-TR spectra of g-CDs fabricated by different amino acids (Fig. S4D), peaks at 870 cm−1 and 710 cm−1 in CDs-16 attributed to C–H of the benzene ring, while CDs-11 displayed N–H and C–N stretching at 650 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1, which associated with the histidine imidazole group. Consequently, CDs-11 synthesized at a hydrothermal temperature of 190 °C for 9 h, and with a mannose to histidine of 7:1, were selected for further experiments.

FimH was a two-domain protein at the tip of type I fimbriae that recognized terminal mannose on glycoproteins [22]. ConA, a native tetramer with specific recognition for mannose, was employed to simulate the binding of FimH on S. typhimurium in conjunction with CDs-11. As shown in Fig. 2G, electrostatic interactions among CDs-11, S. typhimurium and ConA were investigated, revealing zeta potential of −12.2 ± 1.3 mV for CDs-11, –12.5 ± 3.5 mV for S. typhimurium, and 9.5 ± 2.1 mV for ConA. This indicated that both CDs-11 and S. typhimurium carry negative charges, whereas ConA was positively charged, thus partially alleviating the electrostatic repulsion between CDs-11 and S. typhimurium. The binding affinity of CDs-11 and CDs-10 to S. typhimurium was characterized by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC).The dissociation constant (KD) of CDs-11 and Con A was determined to be 25.4 μM (Fig. 3 A), which was approximately 1/20 of that of α-D-mannopyranose (470 μM) [23]. The KD of CDs-10 prepared from pure mannose was also lower than that of α-D-mannopyranose at 135.0 μM (Fig. 3B), likely attributable to the multivalent effect conferred by the presence of numerous mannose residues. However, compared to CDs-10, the dissociation constant of CDs-11 was 1/5, potentially due to the protective effect of amino acid doping on certain sugar groups of the CDs-11. This result suggested that amino acid incorporation enhanced the binding affinity of g-CDs to Con A. Additionally, it was observed that CDs-11 exhibited a good selectivity toward S. typhimurium compared to other Gram-positive (i.e., S. aureus) and Gram-negative bacteria (i.e., E. coli, P. aeruginosa and V. parahaemolyticus). Notably, after CDs-11 combined with S. typhimurium, the fluorescence intensity weakened by 802 a.u., which was significantly higher than that of other bacteria (177 a.u. for E. coli, 151 a.u. for S. aureus, 362 a.u. for P. aeruginosa, and 60 a.u. for V. parahaemolyticus). Furthermore, when CDs-11 was co-cultured with S. typhimurium and other bacteria, the fluorescence intensity change was not significantly disturbed (851 a.u. for S. typhimurium mixed with E. coli, 804 a.u. for S. typhimurium mixed with S. aureus, 912 a.u. for S. typhimurium mixed with P. aeruginosa, and 945 a.u. for S. typhimurium mixed with V. parahaemolyticus), and the influence of other bacteria was within 17.8 %, which proved that CDs-11 had good specificity for S. typhimurium (Fig. 2F).

The synthesized CDs-11 was characterized as uniform, monodisperse spheres with a mean diameter of 4.75 ± 0.10 nm (Fig. 4 C and D). High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 4C) showed distinct lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) crystalline plane of graphite, thereby confirming a certain crystallinity of CDs-11. Fluorescence properties of CDs-11 were evaluated through excitation, emission and decay spectra (Fig. 4A). The CDs-11 solution exhibited a pale-yellow color in daylight and emitted bright blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light (inset in Fig. 4A). Notably, the emitted fluorescence was influenced by the excitation wavelength, with an increase from 425 nm to 465 nm, indicating a relatively weak excitation dependence and homogeneity in properties. The optimal excitation wavelength was determined to be 360 nm, with an emission peak at 430 nm, as corroborated by two-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4B). The relative fluorescence quantum yield of CDs-11 was measured at 7.02 %, using quinine sulfate as standard (Fig. S4C). The fluorescence decay curve (Fig. 4E) was fitted to a bi-exponential model: I(t) = 1687exp(-t/1.47) + 1272exp(-t/6.01), yielding an average fluorescence lifetime of 4.90 ns. Furthermore, no significant changes in fluorescence intensity were observed after 90 days of storage at room temperature without light protection, demonstrating the excellent long-term stability of CDs-11 (Fig. S5). The elemental chemical states were analyzed via XPS. The survey spectra (Fig. 4F) confirmed the presence of C, N and O elements in CDs-11, with the existence of N indicating the successful combination and reaction of mannose and histidine. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4G) could be divided into three peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C), 286.3 eV (C–O/C–N) and 288.7 eV (CO). In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4H), two peaks were observed at 532.3 eV (C–O–C/C–OH) and 533.7 eV (CO) [24–26]. High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 4I) revealed peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and N–H bonds in CDs-11, respectively. The predominant N species, pyrrolic N, formed through the dehydration reaction of carboxyl and amine groups, was believed to enhance electron cloud density on the CDs-11, thereby improving luminescence efficiency [27].

To construct a fluorescence recovery probe, graphene oxide (GO) was selected as the fluorescence quenching agent. Prior to loading the CDs-11 onto GO, the surface of GO was modified through boronation, enabling the grafting of g-CDs via diol bonds. This grafting resulted in the formation of borate bonds. Upon binding with the target bacteria, these borate bonds were broken, leading to the restoration of fluorescence in the g-CDs. The insets of Fig. 5 C illustrate a visual representation of GO and GO-PBA. Following the reaction with 3-APBA, GO exhibited a distinct darkening. TEM image (Fig. 5A) reveals that GO-PBA presented a large area of thin, wavy sheets, which promoted efficient loading of CDs-11. Elemental mapping (Fig. 5B) confirmed a significant presence of C, O, and B elements on the surface of GO-PBA, indicating successful modification with 3-APBA. In the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 5C), the absorption peak of GO was observed at 228 nm, attributed to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic C–C bonds, while a peak at 295 nm signified the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds [28]. Upon reaction with 3-APBA, the π→π∗ transition was red-shifted from 228 nm to 235 nm, indicating an expansion of electron conjugation due to the grafted benzene ring. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 5D), the new peak at 700 cm−1 in GO-PBA were attributed to out-of-plane ring deformation vibrations of the 1,3-disubstituted benzene ring of 3-APBA. The new peak at 1170 cm−1 in GO-PBA corresponded to the B–OH stretching vibration of 3-APBA. Furthermore, the CO stretching peak at 1720 cm−1 associated with the acid group in GO was absent upon binding to 3-APBA. This likely indicated the substitution of the carboxyl group [16].

After loading CDs-11 onto GO-PBA, TEM image (Fig. 6 A) displayed dark patches on the smooth GO-PBA lamellae, indicating a uniform attachment of CDs-11. EDS mapping images of CDs/GO-PBA (Fig. 6B) confirmed a high distribution of N elements, proving the successful loading of nitrogen-containing CDs-11 onto the surface of GO-PBA. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 6C) showed that CDs-11 exhibited a broad absorption peak at near 265 nm, resulting from the π→π∗ transition in the aromatic sp2 domain of the carbon core of CDs-11 [29,30]. Both GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA displayed absorption bands around 230 nm and 295 nm, attributed to π→π∗ transition in aromatic C–C bonds and n→π∗ transition in CO bonds [28]. Upon interaction with CDs-11, the π→π∗ transition exhibited a red-shifted from 265 nm to 295 nm, reflecting expanded electronic conjugation. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 6D) further confirmed the successful grafting of CDs-11 to GO-PBA. Absorption peaks at 1396 cm−1 were consistent with C–OH stretching vibrations in both GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA, suggesting a surface rich in oxygen functional groups. Notably, CDs/GO-PBA showed a new peak at 1048 cm−1, attributed to C–O–B bonding, indicating a successful reaction between the diol groups on GO-PBA and CDs-11 [31,32].

XPS was employed to further understand the compositional and structural changes. As shown in Fig. 6E, XPS survey spectra indicated the presence of C, N, O and B in both GO-PBA and CDs/GO-PBA. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of GO-PBA (Fig. 6F) could be divided into five peaks of 283.7 eV (C–B), 284.8 eV (C–C), 285.5 eV (C–N), 287.0 eV (C–O) and 289.0 eV (CO) [33,34]. Similarly, the C1s spectrum of CDs/GO-PBA exhibited these peaks: 283.7 eV (C–B), 284.7 eV (C–C), 285.5 eV (C–N), 286.7 eV (C–O) and 288.8 eV (CO). The observed shifts indicated a change in the local chemical environment of carbon. Specifically, the binding energy of C1s in CDs/GO-PBA shifted to a lower value, suggesting electron transfer from CDs-11 to GO-PBA after grafting. The O1s spectrum of GO-PBA (Fig. 6G) could be divided into three peaks at 531.3 eV (CO), 532.7 eV (C–O) and 533.9 eV (O–H). In the O1s spectrum of CDs/GO-PBA, the peaks at 530.9 eV (CO), 532.4 eV (C–O) and 533.6 eV (O–H) were noted [35]. The N1s spectrum of GO-PBA (Fig. 6H) exhibited three peaks at 399.4, 400.7 and 401.6 eV, associated with pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N. The N1s spectrum of CDs/GO-PBA (Fig. 6H) could be divided into three peaks of 399.4 eV (pyridinic N), 400.8 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.7 eV (graphitic N), with only minor shifts in binding energy after the interaction with CDs-11 [36]. The peak at 191.1 eV in GO-PBA (Fig. 6I) corresponded to C–B–(OH)2. In the B1s spectrum of CDs/GO-PBA (Fig. 6I), the peak at 190.8 eV was attributed to C–B–O, indicating a potential transition from C–B–(OH)2 to C–B–O, thus confirming the successful binding of CDs-11 to GO-PBA through borate bond. These results demonstrate the successful modification of CDs-11 on GO-PBA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of S. typhimurium by CDs/GO-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: The detection principle of the CDs/GO-PBA fluorescent probe was illustrated in Fig. 7 A. CDs-11 were attached to GO-PBA through dynamic borate bonding, leading to fluorescence quenching via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) due to weak π-π stacking interactions between CDs-11 and GO-PBA, resulting in a fluorescence “OFF” state. Upon the introduction of S. typhimurium, the borate bonds were disrupted through competitive carbohydrate-lectin binding interactions, facilitating the detachment of CDs-11 from the GO surface. Once released, the distance between CDs-11 and GO-PBA increased, significantly reducing the quenching effect on CDs-11 fluorescence and resulting in a transition to a fluorescence “ON” state.

As shown in Fig. 7B, the CDs/GO-PBA solution exhibited negligible fluorescence prior to the addition of S. typhimurium. However, upon addition, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm increased by sixfold, indicating that this fluorescent turn-on probe was effective for detecting S. typhimurium. The impact of incubation time on fluorescence intensity was also examined (Fig. 7C). Fluorescence intensity increased reaching a maximum at 1 h, before decreasing with extended incubation time. This trend may result from the time required for CDs-11 to dissociate from GO-PBA and bind to S. typhimurium. Prolonging the incubation beyond 1 h may decrease fluorescence intensity due to the uptake of CDs-11 by the bacteria. Consequently, an incubation period of 1 h was chosen as optimal. As shown in Fig. 7F, numerous S. typhimurium cells were observed on the surface of CDs/GO-PBA, confirming their adsorption during the detection process. Confocal fluorescence microscopy images (Fig. 7G) revealed blue fluorescence recovery from CDs-11 confined to the regions of the graphene surface (indicated by the black area in the right panel of Fig. 7G).

Fig. 7D shows the fluorescence spectra of the CDs/GO-PBA-bacteria complexes under 360 nm excitation, demonstrating that fluorescence intensity gradually increased with rising S. typhimurium concentrations. A linear quantitative relationship was established between S. typhimurium concentration and fluorescence intensity at 430 nm across the range of 104–107 CFU mL−1, yielding the linear equation I430 = 44.3 LgC (S. typhimurium) + 128.1 (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 7E). The limit of detection (LOD) for S. typhimurium was determined to be 2370 CFU mL−1.

To assess the reliability of the constructed fluorescent probe for practical applications, standard addition experiments methods were conducted on real samples. Chicken samples were spiked with various concentrations of S. typhimurium (104–107 CFU mL−1) and analyzed using the CDs/GO-PBA fluorescent sensor. The analytical results, presented in Table 2 , demonstrated recoveries ranging from 93.59 % to 102.54 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 1.50 % (n = 3). It showed that there was no significant difference between the results for contaminated chicken samples and standard bacterial samples, affirming the potential of method for analyzing real samples.

Table 3 exhibits the comparisons of the proposed sensor with recently reported detection methods for S. typhimurium. The CDs/GO-PBA sensor has a lower LOD (2370 CFU mL−1) than the QCM method for S. typhimurium. Compared to other highly sensitive methods, such as colorimetric and DNA-AgNCs-fluorescent sensor, this proposed sensor shows the merit of simplicity and short-time, as it requires only 1 h for incubation and fluorescence detection. Furthermore, compared to the Aptamer-Fe3O4@Au fluorescence method, the proposed sensor exhibits a broader linear response range, spanning from 104 to 107 CFU mL−1. Unlike electrochemical methods, which require complex systems and electronic integrated components, this approach is straightforward and avoids such requirements.

In this study, CDs/GO-PBA, a novel fluorescence-recoverable sensor, was successfully developed using g-CDs and GO-PBA for the detection of S. typhimurium. The incorporation of histidine protected sugar groups of g-CDs and facilitated the formation of a glycopeptide-mimetic structure, enhancing the affinity of CDs/GO-PBA for lectins. Furthermore, CDs/GO-PBA exhibited an excellent fluorescence signal response to S. typhimurium. The g-CDs demonstrated remarkable fluorescence stability, maintaining their properties for over 90 days at room temperature without light protection. Consequently, the CDs/GO-PBA displayed high sensitivity, stability and strong recognition affinity for S. typhimurium. The developed CDs/GO-PBA fluorescent sensor was capable of detecting S. typhimurium within 104–107 CFU mL−1 concentration range and identified concentrations as low as 2370 CFU mL−1 with a recovery rate ranging from 93 % to 103 % in chicken. This fluorescent biosensor holds significant potential for the detection of foodborne pathogens.

Xuan Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhenhao Lin: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Jihai Cai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xiaoying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22208114), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2022M721195) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2024A1515011818).
